title: Challenge: Constraint
description: Generates a formal writing constraint to obey for the whole piece.
entries: Every sentence must be shorter than the one before it.
Never use the letter 'e'.
Write the entire scene as dialogue, with no narration at all.
Use no adjectives anywhere.
Begin every paragraph with the same word.
Tell the whole story backward, from ending to opening.
Write it in exactly one hundred words, no more, no less.
Never name your protagonist.
Use only single-syllable words.
Do not use the word 'and' at any point.
Set the entire piece inside a single unbroken minute.
Write it as a letter that will never be sent.
Never describe anyone's face.
Include no interior thoughts; show everything from the outside.
Write the whole thing in the second person.
Let no sentence contain a verb in the past tense.
Make every line of dialogue a question.
Confine the entire story to one room the characters cannot leave.
Use no proper nouns of any kind.
Write it as a list, and let the list tell the story.
Never use a comma.
Have your narrator lie at least three times without flagging it.
Write the scene twice on the page: once as it happened, once as it was remembered.
Use only sentences of exactly five words.
Do not use any form of the verb 'to be'.
Tell the story entirely through what the characters refuse to say.
Make color the only thing you are allowed to describe.
Write it as a set of instructions the reader is meant to follow.
Include a sentence that is also, read alone, the whole story in miniature.
Never use dialogue tags; let the reader work out who is speaking.
Write the piece so it reads the same forward and, section by section, backward.
Give every character exactly one line of speech and no more.
Anchor each paragraph to a different one of the five senses, in order.
Write the climax first, then earn it in the paragraphs that follow.
Use no punctuation except the period.
Let the weather do all the emotional work; the people stay flat.
Write it as a transcript of a recording with three seconds missing.
Never let two characters be in the same place at the same time.
Repeat one sentence, unchanged, at the start, middle, and end.
Tell a story of great loss without ever using a sad word.
